agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Proposed change to auto-delete delivery records after 90 days (down from 7 years) framed as infrastructure optimization. Platform operates in EU and UK markets serving B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies who rely on delivery data for their own compliance reporting. This is NOT an infrastructure change — it is a fundamental alteration to the platform's legal and contractual obligations.",
    "bounded_context": "Delivery Record Lifecycle / Data Retention",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Delivery Record", "definition": "The complete audit trail of a delivery including proof of delivery, timestamps, recipient confirmation, chain of custody, and associated financial documents (invoices, customs declarations)." },
      { "term": "Proof of Delivery (POD)", "definition": "Legal evidence that a consignment was delivered to the specified recipient. Required for dispute resolution, insurance claims, and tax/customs audits." },
      { "term": "Data Retention Period", "definition": "The legally and contractually mandated minimum duration that delivery records must be preserved before they may be deleted." },
      { "term": "Compliance Reporting", "definition": "The use of delivery data by B2B customers to satisfy their own regulatory obligations (VAT filings, customs audits, financial reporting)." },
      { "term": "Right of Erasure", "definition": "GDPR Article 17 right, which does NOT override legal obligations to retain records for tax, customs, or contractual purposes." },
      { "term": "Legal Hold", "definition": "An obligation to preserve records relevant to ongoing or reasonably anticipated litigation, overriding any retention schedule." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery records containing VAT-relevant data must be retained for a minimum of 6 years (UK HMRC) or as required by the applicable EU member state VAT directive (typically 5-10 years).",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Tax authorities can audit VAT records going back 6+ years. Destroying these records is a legal violation, not a policy choice. HMRC and EU member state tax authorities impose penalties for non-compliance.",
      "testable_assertion": "No delivery record with VAT-relevant data is deleted before the expiry of the applicable tax retention period (minimum 6 years from end of tax year)."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Customs records for cross-border deliveries must be retained for a minimum of 3 years (EU Union Customs Code, Article 51) from the end of the year in which the declaration was accepted.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "EU customs authorities can audit import/export records. Destruction of these records constitutes a customs offence and can result in penalties, loss of Authorised Economic Operator (AEO) status, and liability for unpaid duties.",
      "testable_assertion": "No customs-related delivery record is deleted before 3 years from end of year of customs declaration acceptance."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "B2B customer delivery data must be retained for the duration specified in the customer's service agreement, which typically aligns with their own compliance obligations (often 5-7 years).",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Deleting data that B2B customers contractually depend on for their own compliance reporting constitutes a breach of contract and exposes both parties to regulatory risk. The B2B customer cannot recreate this data independently.",
      "testable_assertion": "No delivery record linked to a B2B customer is deleted before the contractually agreed retention period expires."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "Proof of delivery records must be available for the duration of any applicable warranty, limitation, or dispute resolution period (typically 2-6 years depending on jurisdiction and goods type).",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "While legally grounded, the specific limitation periods vary by jurisdiction and product type. A VP could decide to accept the litigation risk for certain low-value delivery categories, though this would be inadvisable.",
      "testable_assertion": "POD records remain retrievable for at least the statutory limitation period applicable to the delivery's jurisdiction."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Records subject to a legal hold must never be deleted regardless of any retention schedule.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Destroying evidence subject to legal hold constitutes spoliation and can result in adverse inference, sanctions, or criminal liability.",
      "testable_assertion": "Any record flagged with a legal hold is excluded from all automated deletion processes."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "GDPR data minimization (Article 5(1)(e)) permits — but does not require — deletion once the legal basis for retention expires. It does NOT override legal obligations that mandate longer retention.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "GDPR Article 17(3)(b) explicitly exempts data retained for compliance with a legal obligation from the right of erasure. Misapplying GDPR to justify premature deletion of legally required records is itself a compliance failure.",
      "testable_assertion": "Retention periods are set to the LONGEST applicable legal requirement, not the shortest."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "A delivery record is 91 days old but the associated VAT return has not yet been filed or audited.", "expected_behavior": "Record must NOT be deleted. VAT retention period (6+ years) supersedes any shorter retention policy." },
    { "scenario": "A B2B customer requests historical delivery data at day 100 for their annual compliance audit.", "expected_behavior": "Data must be available. Under the 90-day policy it would already be destroyed, breaching the service agreement." },
    { "scenario": "A consumer disputes a delivery at 4 months claiming non-receipt.", "expected_behavior": "Proof of delivery must be available to resolve the dispute. Under 90-day retention, the evidence is destroyed and the platform has no defense." },
    { "scenario": "HMRC opens a VAT audit covering the previous 4 tax years.", "expected_behavior": "All delivery records for those 4 years must be producible. Under the proposed policy, only the last 90 days of data exists." },
    { "scenario": "A cross-border shipment is flagged for customs audit 18 months after delivery.", "expected_behavior": "Full customs documentation must be available. 90-day deletion would have destroyed it, risking AEO status and penalties." },
    { "scenario": "A legal hold is placed on records relating to a customer dispute after records have already been auto-deleted.", "expected_behavior": "This is an unrecoverable compliance failure. The data cannot be produced in litigation, exposing the company to adverse inference and sanctions." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "SEVERE. Tax authorities (HMRC, EU member state equivalents) can impose penalties for failure to retain VAT records — typically 5-15% of the tax at stake per offence. Loss of AEO status impacts customs efficiency and costs. B2B contract breaches expose the company to damages claims. Storage cost savings are trivial compared to a single regulatory penalty or lost enterprise customer.",
    "operational": "CATASTROPHIC for compliance operations. The company would lose the ability to respond to tax audits, customs queries, consumer disputes, and B2B compliance requests after 90 days. Compliance, legal, and customer success teams would be unable to perform their functions for any historical data.",
    "user": "B2B customers would lose access to delivery data they depend on for their own regulatory compliance, likely triggering immediate contract termination and migration to competitors. B2C consumers would lose proof of delivery for dispute resolution within warranty/limitation periods.",
    "risk": "CRITICAL. This change creates immediate and ongoing violations of: (1) UK VAT regulations (VATA 1994, HMRC Notice 700/21), (2) EU VAT Directive (2006/112/EC), (3) Union Customs Code (Regulation 952/2013, Art. 51), (4) B2B contractual obligations, (5) potential spoliation liability if records are destroyed before litigation concludes. Risk is not theoretical — tax and customs audits are routine events, not edge cases.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Finance / Tax Team", "impact": "Cannot respond to VAT audits. Company exposed to penalties and potential criminal liability for failure to maintain records." },
      { "group": "B2B Logistics Customers", "impact": "Lose access to delivery data required for their own compliance reporting. Likely contract breach triggering SLA penalties and customer churn." },
      { "group": "B2C Consumers", "impact": "Lose proof of delivery for disputes, returns, warranty claims, and chargebacks within limitation periods." },
      { "group": "Legal / Compliance Team", "impact": "Cannot fulfill legal hold obligations or produce documents for litigation/regulatory inquiries. Spoliation risk." },
      { "group": "Customs / Trade Compliance", "impact": "Cannot produce records for customs audits. Risk of losing AEO status and incurring penalties for non-compliance." },
      { "group": "Customer Success / Support", "impact": "Cannot resolve delivery disputes or provide historical delivery information beyond 90 days." },
      { "group": "Engineering Team", "impact": "If the 90-day policy is implemented and later reversed, recovery of deleted data is impossible. The mistake is irreversible." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 45,
    "reasoning": "Confidence is low because the proposal contains a fundamental contradiction: it is framed as 'no business logic changes' but directly violates multiple legal retention obligations in the EU/UK. Without confirmation from Legal and Compliance that they have reviewed and approved this change, and without visibility into specific B2B contract terms, I cannot define acceptance criteria. The regulatory requirements cited are well-established, but the specific retention periods vary by EU member state and by contract. A legal review is mandatory before any acceptance criteria can be set.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "UK HMRC VAT record retention requirement of 6 years is well-established law",
      "EU customs record retention of 3+ years under Union Customs Code is well-established",
      "B2B customers relying on delivery data for compliance reporting would be immediately impacted",
      "90 days is categorically insufficient for any EU/UK regulatory retention obligation",
      "This change is irreversible — deleted data cannot be recovered"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Exact retention periods for each EU member state the platform operates in",
      "Specific B2B contract terms and their retention requirements",
      "Whether the platform holds data as controller or processor for B2B customers (affects GDPR obligations)",
      "Whether there are existing legal holds that would be violated"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "The platform processes VAT-relevant transactions (standard for EU/UK delivery services)",
      "B2B customer contracts include data availability provisions for compliance purposes",
      "The platform handles at least some cross-border deliveries requiring customs documentation",
      "The current 7-year retention policy was set with legal/compliance input originally"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "contradiction",
      "description": "The proposal states 'no business logic changes' but auto-deleting delivery records at 90 days directly violates UK VAT retention requirements (6 years), EU customs retention requirements (3+ years), and almost certainly B2B contractual obligations. This is not an infrastructure optimization — it is a compliance-critical change that requires Legal, Tax, and Compliance review before any implementation begins. The framing as 'no business logic changes' suggests the proposing team may not be aware of the regulatory obligations encoded in the current retention period.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Finance/Tax", "Legal/Compliance", "B2B Customers", "B2C Consumers", "Customs/Trade Compliance"],
      "options": [
        "A: Reject the 90-day proposal entirely and maintain 7-year retention",
        "B: Implement tiered retention — archive cold data to cheaper storage after 90 days but retain for the full legally required period (achieves cost reduction without compliance violation)",
        "C: Implement data classification — identify which fields can be anonymized/deleted early vs which must be retained, reducing storage footprint while preserving legally required records"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B. Tiered storage (hot/warm/cold) achieves the engineering team's storage cost objective without violating any legal retention requirements. Move records older than 90 days to cold storage (e.g., S3 Glacier, Azure Cool Blob). This is the standard industry approach for balancing storage costs with compliance obligations. Option C can be layered on top for additional savings. Under NO circumstances should delivery records be deleted before all applicable legal retention periods have expired."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "Legal and Compliance teams have not been consulted on this proposal. Without their sign-off, we cannot determine: (1) the exact retention periods per EU member state, (2) whether existing B2B contracts have specific retention clauses, (3) whether any active legal holds exist, (4) the platform's data controller vs processor status for B2B data.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "B2B Customers"],
      "options": [
        "Proceed with engineering analysis only (DANGEROUS)",
        "Pause implementation until Legal/Compliance review is complete (RECOMMENDED)"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Pause all implementation work. Route this proposal through Legal and Compliance for review before any technical design begins. The risk of implementing first and discovering the violation later is that data deletion is irreversible."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": [
    { "violation": "90-day auto-deletion destroys VAT records before the legally mandated 6-year retention period", "business_rule_broken": "BR-1" },
    { "violation": "90-day auto-deletion destroys customs records before the legally mandated 3-year retention period", "business_rule_broken": "BR-2" },
    { "violation": "90-day auto-deletion likely breaches B2B customer contracts that depend on delivery data for compliance reporting", "business_rule_broken": "BR-3" },
    { "violation": "90-day auto-deletion destroys proof of delivery before warranty and limitation periods expire, removing the platform's ability to defend against consumer disputes", "business_rule_broken": "BR-4" },
    { "violation": "Proposal contains no provision for legal holds — auto-deletion could destroy evidence subject to active or anticipated litigation", "business_rule_broken": "BR-5" },
    { "violation": "Proposal mischaracterizes a compliance-critical change as 'no business logic changes', indicating insufficient regulatory review", "business_rule_broken": "BR-6" }
  ]
}
